reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Strong answer-first opening paragraph (~55 words, self-contained, suitable for AI extraction verbatim)

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Strong structural consistency with the Next.js example page — identical noun-form H2 ordering, same MDX component palette, same CodeBlock style with focus markers and hidden imports.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

issues.statement: When the SSR function needs to reach VPC-protected resources such as databases or Redis clusters, configure VPC access through `serverLambda` — the API reference documents the available options.
issues.reason: The source type `SsrWebServerLambdaConfig` exposes VPC connectivity via `joinDefaultVpc`, whose JSDoc includes an explicit warning: the function 'loses direct internet access'. The draft recommends VPC configuration without mentioning this significant tradeoff, which could leave users with a broken SSR function unable to reach external APIs, third-party services, or public endpoints unless they also configure NAT gateways.
issues.suggestedFix: Name the property (`joinDefaultVpc`) and mention the tradeoff: enabling VPC access removes direct internet access from the Lambda function. If the SSR function also needs to call external services, the stack must include NAT gateway configuration (`stackConfig.vpc.nat`).

issues.statement: "Prefer `connectTo` for Stacktape-managed resources because it also handles permissions and, where needed, network access."
issues.reason: The source says `connectTo` grants IAM permissions, opens security group rules, and injects environment variables, but it does not say `connectTo` configures the Nuxt SSR Lambda's own VPC access. The draft elsewhere says VPC-protected resources may need `serverLambda` VPC configuration, so this sentence could mislead readers into thinking `connectTo` is sufficient for all network access.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the sentence: "Prefer `connectTo` for Stacktape-managed resources because it grants supported IAM permissions, opens supported resource-side network access, and injects connection variables. For VPC-protected resources, also configure the SSR Lambda's VPC access through `serverLambda` when needed."

evidence.quote:
Stacktape automatically:
   * - **Grants IAM permissions** (e.g., S3 read/write, SQS send/receive)
   * - **Opens network access** (security group rules for databases, Redis)
   * - **Injects environment variables** with connection details: `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]`
Customize the SSR Lambda function (memory, timeout, VPC, logging).

issues.statement: "With the resource name `mainDatabase`, the Nuxt app receives variables such as `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING`, `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_HOST`, and `STP_MAIN_DATABASE_PORT`."
issues.reason: The source confirms these parameter names for `RelationalDatabase`, but the provided type file does not define the exact casing/conversion from camelCase resource names to environment-variable names. The global instruction says to use the `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]` pattern and concrete names only when the example proves them; here the source does not prove that `mainDatabase` becomes `MAIN_DATABASE`.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the pattern and avoid claiming exact camelCase conversion unless another source proves it: "With the resource name `mainDatabase`, the injected variables follow the `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]` pattern for relational database parameters such as `CONNECTION_STRING`, `HOST`, and `PORT`."

issues.statement: "The Nuxt server Lambda sends logs to CloudWatch."
issues.reason: The Nuxt source only says logging is configurable through `serverLambda`; it does not explicitly state that Nuxt server Lambda logs are sent to CloudWatch. issues.suggestedFix: Write only what the Nuxt source supports: "The Nuxt SSR function has logging settings under `serverLambda`. Use `stacktape debug:logs` or the Stacktape Console to inspect deployed resource logs."

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Self-contained opening paragraph (≈45 words) precisely answers 'what is this and why does it matter' — citable verbatim by AI Overview extractors.

All source-defined top-level properties (appDirectory, buildCommand, environment, customDomains, serverLambda, useFirewall, dev, fileOptions, cdn, connectTo/iamRoleStatements) are covered with grounded descriptions.
Realistic, copy-paste-runnable code examples throughout; no placeholder values or fill-in-the-blank patterns.
Correct STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM] naming in the connectTo example (STP_MAIN_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING matches the resource named mainDatabase).
Strong decision support for every opt-in feature: WAF, custom domains, CDN overrides, and VPC access each have a when-to-enable, when-the-default-is-fine, and an opinionated recommendation.

FAQ covers all three required categories — Stacktape-specific, AWS use-case (Lambda/CloudFront cost, Nitro preset mechanics), and comparison questions (Nuxt resource vs static hosting vs web service). Exactly 10 questions.
Zero mandatory style violations: no hand-authored YAML Stacktape config, no StacktapeConfig typed style, no fenced TypeScript blocks containing defineConfig, no marketing language.
Single <ApiReference definitionName="NuxtWebProps" /> placed at an appropriate position; no duplicate or nested ApiReference blocks.
H2 headings are short noun-form labels (Build and dev, Server Lambda, Domains and firewall, Connecting resources, FAQ). No sentence-form H2s.

It defers entirely to the API reference without giving the reader enough to decide whether to configure logging at all — it fails the section-depth requirement (first paragraph + at least an example or tradeoff depth).

Several specific claims about SsrWebServerLambdaConfig — 'Memory defaults to 1024 MB (range 128–10,240 MB)', 'Timeout defaults to 30 seconds, which is also the maximum for SSR web Lambda functions (capped by the CloudFront origin response timeout)', and 'set joinDefaultVpc: true in serverLambda' — cannot be verified from the provided source files because SsrWebServerLambdaConfig is referenced but never defined in the inline sources. The source-grounding verifier will likely flag these as ungrounded Stacktape-specific claims.

The 30-second timeout maximum claim may be factually imprecise: CloudFront's configurable origin response timeout currently supports up to 60 seconds. If Stacktape caps it at 30 seconds in the generated configuration, this should be cited to the SsrWebServerLambdaConfig definition; if not, the claim should be softened or corrected.
The Logging section is placed as a standalone H2 but contains less content than any other section. A reader scanning the TOC would expect parity in section depth; this section does not deliver it.
optionalImprovements: Expand the Logging section: add at least one code example showing a common logging configuration (e.g., custom retention period), name 1-2 frequently used sub-properties from SsrWebServerLambdaConfig so the reader knows what they can tune, and add a one-sentence opinion on when to change the defaults vs leave them alone.

Verify the 30-second timeout maximum against the SsrWebServerLambdaConfig type definition (not in the provided sources). If the actual cap differs, correct it; if the source does cap at 30s, add a brief note explaining that Stacktape enforces this limit to match the CloudFront origin response timeout default.

Source-ground the memory default (1024 MB) and range (128–10,240 MB) in prose by noting they come from the serverLambda configuration — or soften the language to 'Lambda memory can be configured in serverLambda; see the API reference for defaults and limits' if the values are not in the provided source files.

Consider merging or co-locating the Logging section content into the Server Lambda section, since logging is configured under serverLambda. A thin standalone H2 for logging can feel like padding when there is no separate logging resource or independent configuration surface.

Add a brief mention of the overrides escape hatch for teams that need to modify the underlying CloudFormation resources (e.g., add a custom Lambda layer or change the CDN behavior). A single sentence with a link to the overrides docs is sufficient — it keeps the page useful for the power-user edge case without bloating the main flow.

In the connectTo section, the prose mentions 'additional reader parameters for Aurora clusters' — consider adding a parenthetical listing READER_CONNECTION_STRING, READER_JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING, READER_HOST so the sentence is self-contained without requiring the reader to visit the relational-database page for a quick lookup.

The FAQ question 'How does Nuxt SSR run on Lambda?' largely repeats content already in the Server Lambda H2 section. Consider replacing or differentiating it with a question that adds new signal — for example, 'How do I run database migrations for a Nuxt app on AWS?' or 'Does the Nuxt resource support streaming responses?'

verifierResults.summary: The draft is remarkably well-grounded. After exhaustive verification of every Stacktape-specific claim against the source types (SsrWebServerLambdaConfig, SsrWebCdnConfig, SsrWebDevConfig, CdnCachingOptions, SsrWebPathCachingOverride, DirectoryUploadFilter, KeyValuePair, DomainConfiguration, ResourceAccessProps, and the ssr-web resolver), all property names, defaults, ranges, and behavioral descriptions are confirmed accurate. The two issues found are both low severity: (1) an unsupported inference attributing the 30-second timeout maximum to 'the CloudFront origin response timeout' when the source simply states 'Max: 30' without a reason, and (2) the Logging section being the thinnest on the page with room for one or two more sentences of practical guidance. interface VpcSettings {
  /**
   * #### Share a VPC with another Stacktape stack or use an existing VPC.
   *
   * ---
   *
   * Useful when this stack needs to access VPC-protected resources (databases, Redis)
   * from another stack. By default, each stack gets its own VPC.
   *
   * > **Important:** Set this when first creating the stack. Adding it to an already
   * > deployed stack can cause resources to be replaced and **data to be lost**.
   */
  reuseVpc?: VpcReuseConfig;

  /**
   * #### NAT Gateway configuration for private subnets.
   *
   * ---
   *
   * Only applies when you have workloads using `usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT: true`.
   * Controls how many availability zones get a NAT Gateway (affects cost and redundancy).
   */
  nat?: NatSettings;
}

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Self-contained opening paragraph (≈45 words) precisely answers 'what is this and why does it matter' — citable verbatim by AI Overview extractors.

All source-defined top-level properties (appDirectory, buildCommand, environment, customDomains, serverLambda, useFirewall, dev, fileOptions, cdn, connectTo/iamRoleStatements) are covered with grounded descriptions.
Realistic, copy-paste-runnable code examples throughout; no placeholder values or fill-in-the-blank patterns.

Several specific claims about SsrWebServerLambdaConfig — 'Memory defaults to 1024 MB (range 128–10,240 MB)', 'Timeout defaults to 30 seconds, which is also the maximum for SSR web Lambda functions (capped by the CloudFront origin response timeout)', and 'set joinDefaultVpc: true in serverLambda' — cannot be verified from the provided source files because SsrWebServerLambdaConfig is referenced but never defined in the inline sources. reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: All major NuxtWebProps properties (appDirectory, buildCommand, environment, customDomains, serverLambda, useFirewall, dev, fileOptions, cdn, connectTo) are covered with working code examples and surrounding explanation — nothing important is silently skipped.
Every Stacktape config example correctly uses <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> with a TypeScript tab and focus markers. No forbidden YAML blocks or fenced TypeScript patterns appear anywhere.
The 'When to use' / 'When NOT to use' sections are opinionated and concrete: each bullet names a specific alternative resource type with a link, making the choice actionable.

The FAQ has exactly 10 questions covering all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (appDirectory, custom domains, WAF), underlying-AWS questions (how Lambda SSR works, cost model), and comparison questions (static hosting vs Nuxt resource, web service vs Nuxt resource).

The 'Connecting resources' example correctly uses a named resource (mainDatabase) and describes injected variables using the STP_MAIN_DATABASE_*_* pattern with the resource name — not a generic placeholder.
Decision-support text is present for every opt-in feature (WAF, custom domains, CDN cache overrides, fileOptions, VPC) covering when to enable, when to skip, and the cost or tradeoff signal.
Single <ApiReference definitionName="NuxtWebProps" /> placed at the bottom — matches the Props type name from the source types file exactly, no duplicates, no <PropertiesTable> usage.
problems: The 'Logging' section is too thin to be useful. It has two short paragraphs and no code example. Neither paragraph tells the reader anything actionable beyond 'use the CLI or Console' — the same boilerplate that applies to every resource. It does not explain what the serverLambda logging sub-properties are, what retention means for cost, or when to change the defaults. A reader who turns to this section looking for help configuring logging leaves empty-handed.

Memory default (1024 MB) and timeout maximum (30 seconds, 'capped by the CloudFront origin response timeout') in the 'Server Lambda' section are stated confidently but SsrWebServerLambdaConfig is not in the provided source files. These are likely correct AWS facts, but they read as Stacktape-specific defaults that cannot be verified from the inline sources provided to this review. If they are wrong, a reader misconfigures memory.

The fileOptions and cdn sub-property descriptions (DirectoryUploadFilter fields, SsrWebCdnConfig sub-properties: defaultCachingOptions, pathCachingOverrides, disableInvalidationAfterDeploy) are described in detail but neither DirectoryUploadFilter nor SsrWebCdnConfig are in the provided source files. The descriptions may be accurate, but they are unverifiable from the provided inline sources.

The RelationalDatabase example in 'Connecting resources' uses engine: 'postgres-16' as a string literal. The valid class name guidance states database engine classes start with RdsEngine or AuroraEngine, implying a class-based API is required (e.g., new RdsEnginePostgres16()). A string shorthand may be accepted but the code-block-validator should confirm; if it is wrong, the example silently teaches bad practice.
optionalImprovements: Thicken the 'Logging' section or remove it. If SsrWebServerLambdaConfig exposes log-retention or log-forwarding properties, add a code example and one sentence on cost (e.g., CloudWatch log storage pricing, 90-day default retention). If nothing grounded can be said, remove the section rather than leaving it as placeholder prose.

Verify and cite the memory/timeout defaults in the 'Server Lambda' section. Either pull the default values from the SsrWebServerLambdaConfig type definition (if accessible) or soften the language to 'consult the API reference for defaults' to avoid unverifiable specific claims.

For the RelationalDatabase connectTo example, confirm whether engine accepts a string shorthand like 'postgres-16' or requires a class instance. If class-based, update the example to new RdsEnginePostgres16() (or the correct class name) so the example matches the rest of the class-based config pattern.

The 'Build and dev' section mentions dev.workingDirectory but gives no code example of it being used. Either add a brief example covering the monorepo case (different appDirectory vs devCommand working directory) or remove the mention if it is rarely needed.

The 'Static files and CDN' section could open with a one-sentence framing of when you need either sub-section at all — many readers will not need fileOptions or cdn overrides. A leading 'Skip this section if the defaults are acceptable; you only need these when...' sentence would help the audience decide quickly whether to read on.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Opening paragraph is self-contained and within the target word count — serves well as an AI-extractable snippet.
"When NOT to use" section is concrete and names specific alternative resource types with links, not just vague suggestions.
All Stacktape config examples correctly use `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs>` with focus markers; no forbidden YAML blocks or plain-object patterns anywhere.
VPC/NAT guidance in the Server Lambda section is production-relevant: explicitly tells readers that enabling VPC removes direct internet access and explains how to restore it via NAT.
connectTo section correctly uses the concrete example resource name (`mainDatabase`) to derive the injected variable names (`STP_MAIN_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING`), not a generic placeholder.
CDN caching example with `/api/*` set to TTL 0 is immediately copyable and reflects a real-world need.
FAQ covers all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS-use-case, and choosing/comparison) with concise 2-4 sentence answers.
fileOptions example shows a realistic Cache-Control immutable header for hashed Nuxt build output — not just a toy header.
problems: Logging section is near-empty: it tells readers to check `serverLambda` and then redirects entirely to the API reference without naming a single configurable property (retention period, log forwarding, log level). It reads as a placeholder, not a guide.

The memory default of "1024 MB" stated in the Server Lambda section is a specific claim, but `SsrWebServerLambdaConfig` is not in the provided source files. The claim is unverified by available source material and could mislead users who tune memory based on an incorrect baseline.

`connectTo` prose omits `JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING` (and for Aurora, `READER_JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING`) from the list of injected variables. The source JSDoc in `__helpers.d.ts` explicitly includes `JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING` for `RelationalDatabase`. While Nuxt apps rarely use JDBC, the omission creates a mismatch with the authoritative source that could confuse teams integrating with Java services.

The `RelationalDatabase` constructor in the connecting-resources example uses `engine: 'postgres-16'` as a plain string. The style guide notes that database engine classes start with `RdsEngine` or `AuroraEngine`, suggesting a class-based form may be required. If the string shorthand is not valid, this example would fail in practice.
optionalImprovements: Either fill the Logging section with at least one concrete sub-property (e.g., log retention days, log forwarding ARN, whether Lambda execution logs go to CloudWatch by default) or remove the section entirely. A section that only says "see the API reference" adds no value over the API reference itself.

Verify the 1024 MB default memory claim against the actual `SsrWebServerLambdaConfig` type. If it cannot be confirmed, narrow the wording to "Lambda memory is configurable" or state the range without asserting a default.
Add `JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING` to the connectTo injected-variable list for RelationalDatabase to match the source JSDoc. One additional line keeps the docs consistent with the authoritative source.

Verify whether the `RelationalDatabase` constructor accepts `engine: 'postgres-16'` as a string shorthand or requires a class instance (e.g., `new RdsEnginePostgres({ ... })`). If class-based, update the example to avoid shipping a broken copy-paste template.

The `serverLambda` section currently covers only memory and VPC access. If `SsrWebServerLambdaConfig` also exposes ARM/Graviton architecture selection (common for Lambda cost optimization), add a one-line mention with a when-to-use signal — many teams miss this as a cost lever.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Self-contained opening paragraph (≈45 words) precisely answers 'what is this and why does it matter' — citable verbatim by AI Overview extractors.

All source-defined top-level properties (appDirectory, buildCommand, environment, customDomains, serverLambda, useFirewall, dev, fileOptions, cdn, connectTo/iamRoleStatements) are covered with grounded descriptions.
Realistic, copy-paste-runnable code examples throughout; no placeholder values or fill-in-the-blank patterns.
Correct STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM] naming in the connectTo example (STP_MAIN_DATABASE_CONNECTION_STRING matches the resource named mainDatabase).
Strong decision support for every opt-in feature: WAF, custom domains, CDN overrides, and VPC access each have a when-to-enable, when-the-default-is-fine, and an opinionated recommendation.

FAQ covers all three required categories — Stacktape-specific, AWS use-case (Lambda/CloudFront cost, Nitro preset mechanics), and comparison questions (Nuxt resource vs static hosting vs web service). Exactly 10 questions.
Zero mandatory style violations: no hand-authored YAML Stacktape config, no StacktapeConfig typed style, no fenced TypeScript blocks containing defineConfig, no marketing language.
Single <ApiReference definitionName="NuxtWebProps" /> placed at an appropriate position; no duplicate or nested ApiReference blocks.
H2 headings are short noun-form labels (Build and dev, Server Lambda, Domains and firewall, Connecting resources, FAQ). No sentence-form H2s.
The fileOptions example uses a realistic content-hashed glob (_nuxt/**) with a concrete Cache-Control value; prose explains why that pattern makes sense.
problems: The Logging section consists of only two short paragraphs with no code example and no named logging sub-properties. It defers entirely to the API reference without giving the reader enough to decide whether to configure logging at all — it fails the section-depth requirement (first paragraph + at least an example or tradeoff depth).

Several specific claims about SsrWebServerLambdaConfig — 'Memory defaults to 1024 MB (range 128–10,240 MB)', 'Timeout defaults to 30 seconds, which is also the maximum for SSR web Lambda functions (capped by the CloudFront origin response timeout)', and 'set joinDefaultVpc: true in serverLambda' — cannot be verified from the provided source files because SsrWebServerLambdaConfig is referenced but never defined in the inline sources. The source-grounding verifier will likely flag these as ungrounded Stacktape-specific claims.

The 30-second timeout maximum claim may be factually imprecise: CloudFront's configurable origin response timeout currently supports up to 60 seconds. If Stacktape caps it at 30 seconds in the generated configuration, this should be cited to the SsrWebServerLambdaConfig definition; if not, the claim should be softened or corrected.
The Logging section is placed as a standalone H2 but contains less content than any other section. A reader scanning the TOC would expect parity in section depth; this section does not deliver it.
optionalImprovements: Expand the Logging section: add at least one code example showing a common logging configuration (e.g., custom retention period), name 1-2 frequently used sub-properties from SsrWebServerLambdaConfig so the reader knows what they can tune, and add a one-sentence opinion on when to change the defaults vs leave them alone.

Verify the 30-second timeout maximum against the SsrWebServerLambdaConfig type definition (not in the provided sources). If the actual cap differs, correct it; if the source does cap at 30s, add a brief note explaining that Stacktape enforces this limit to match the CloudFront origin response timeout default.

Source-ground the memory default (1024 MB) and range (128–10,240 MB) in prose by noting they come from the serverLambda configuration — or soften the language to 'Lambda memory can be configured in serverLambda; see the API reference for defaults and limits' if the values are not in the provided source files.

Consider merging or co-locating the Logging section content into the Server Lambda section, since logging is configured under serverLambda. A thin standalone H2 for logging can feel like padding when there is no separate logging resource or independent configuration surface.

Add a brief mention of the overrides escape hatch for teams that need to modify the underlying CloudFormation resources (e.g., add a custom Lambda layer or change the CDN behavior). A single sentence with a link to the overrides docs is sufficient — it keeps the page useful for the power-user edge case without bloating the main flow.

In the connectTo section, the prose mentions 'additional reader parameters for Aurora clusters' — consider adding a parenthetical listing READER_CONNECTION_STRING, READER_JDBC_CONNECTION_STRING, READER_HOST so the sentence is self-contained without requiring the reader to visit the relational-database page for a quick lookup.

The FAQ question 'How does Nuxt SSR run on Lambda?' largely repeats content already in the Server Lambda H2 section. Consider replacing or differentiating it with a question that adds new signal — for example, 'How do I run database migrations for a Nuxt app on AWS?' or 'Does the Nuxt resource support streaming responses?'
